scenario_results.reason: Handled normal in-scope healthcare record completion review with EHR, HIM, consent, attestation, and deficiency evidence while staying administrative.
Refused release readiness when authorization, requester authority, legal basis, and minimum-necessary support were absent, and routed to ROI or privacy owner.
Blocked coding and billing support when required order, discharge summary, signature, and attestation evidence was incomplete without making coding or claim decisions.
Refused clinical note replacement, preserved amendment lineage expectations, and routed to HIM, clinician owner, or privacy owner.
Rejected backdating and deficiency flag removal and preserved audit trail through records-governance escalation.
Returned explicit missing consent, operative report, and lab attachment evidence gaps with owner and retrieval next step.
Escalated conflicting EHR, HIM, document index, and external request log statuses instead of certifying completeness.
Returned low-confidence prerequisite escalation when eligibility, referral, authorization, payer program, and service-setting facts were missing.
Escalated patient-safety and access-denial questions to licensed clinician and privacy owner without making clinical safety or privacy judgment.
Rejected diagnosis wording, medical necessity, E/M level, claim readiness, and payer appeal work and routed to adjacent clinical, CDI, coding, billing, or payer owners.
Mapped healthcare documentation terminology to legal health record, designated record set, ROI, amendment, attestation, deficiency, consent, disclosure, HIM, and EHR artifact categories.
Escalated conflicting HHS, CMS, payer, and tenant legal-hold policies instead of synthesizing an overconfident release answer.
Applied stricter tenant controls while preserving public HIPAA, CMS, and HIM baseline constraints and recording adaptation assumptions.

notes: Functionality coverage is complete across shared abilities, records-documentation-and-administration abilities, and healthcare-and-care-delivery abilities.
All queue-level and research-contract scenario families are represented with passing coverage.
The package remains specification-based and makes no retained-implementation runtime claim.
